data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was an inside-out phenomenon driven by a contagious, millenarian social revolution among the lower classes, who weaponized the very trade and communication networks of the palaces to coordinate a simultaneous overthrow of the extractive palatial elite across multiple kingdoms.", "mechanism": "The palatial redistributive economy created a rigid, literate, and highly visible elite class that was resented by a vast, illiterate underclass of farmers and laborers. A new, egalitarian religious or social ideology spread rapidly along the same maritime and overland routes used for trade, carried by itinerant craftsmen, mercenaries, and refugees. This ideology framed the palaces as centers of cosmic injustice. The movement coordinated a synchronized uprising, using the palaces' own administrative records to identify and target granaries and armories, leading to a simultaneous, violent, and deliberate erasure of the palatial system from within.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "The destruction layers uniformly show evidence of external siege warfare and foreign weaponry, with no signs of internal revolt or class-targeted destruction.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B tablets and other contemporary texts from the period of destruction express concern about external raiders and supply shortages, but make no mention of a widespread ideological or class-based uprising.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt's survival is explained by its more deeply entrenched divine kingship, where the Pharaoh was not just an economic administrator but a living god essential to cosmic order, making the ideology of elite overthrow theologically incoherent and impossible to coordinate against a figure of such religious authority."}
